content:
# Genesis Chapter 20 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 11r–11v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_020_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_020_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 15–16 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0019

---

*Abraham Journeys to Gerar — The Second Wife-Sister Episode (vv. 1–2)*

**20:1** And Abraham journeyed [וַיִּסַּע אַבְרָהָם] from there [מִשָּׁם] toward the land of the Negev [אַרְצָה הַנֶּגֶב] and he dwelt [וַיֵּשֶׁב] between Kadesh [בֵּין-קָדֵשׁ] and between Shur [וּבֵין שׁוּר] and he sojourned [וַיָּגָר] in Gerar [בִּגְרָר].

**20:2** And Abraham said [וַיֹּאמֶר אַבְרָהָם] of Sarah his wife [אֶל-שָׂרָה אִשְׁתּוֹ]: She is my sister [אֲחֹתִי הִוא] — and Abimelech [וַיִּשְׁלַח אֲבִימֶלֶךְ] king of Gerar [מֶלֶךְ גְּרָר] sent [וַיִּשְׁלַח] and took [וַיִּקַּח] Sarah [אֶת-שָׂרָה].

---

*God Warns Abimelech in a Dream (vv. 3–7)*

**20:3** And God [וְאֱלֹהִים] came [בָּא] to Abimelech [אֶל-אֲבִימֶלֶךְ] in a dream of the night [בַּחֲלוֹם הַלָּיְלָה] and said to him [וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ]: Look [הִנְּךָ] — you are dead [מֵת] on account of the woman [עַל-הָאִשָּׁה] whom you have taken [אֲשֶׁר-לָקַחְתָּ] — and she is a man's wife [וְהִוא בְּעֻלַת בָּעַל].

**20:4** And Abimelech had not drawn near to her [וַאֲבִימֶלֶךְ לֹא קָרַב אֵלֶיהָ] — and he said [וַיֹּאמַר]: My Lord [אֲדֹנָי] — will you kill even a righteous nation [הֲגוֹי גַּם-צַדִּיק תַּהֲרֹג]?

**20:5** Did not he himself say to me [הֲלֹא הוּא אָמַר-לִי]: She is my sister [אֲחֹתִי הִוא]? And she herself [וְהִיא-גַם-הִוא] — she said [אָמְרָה]: He is my brother [אָחִי הוּא]. In the integrity of my heart [בְּתָם-לְבָבִי] and in the innocence of my hands [וּבְנִקְיֹן כַּפַּי] I have done this [עָשִׂיתִי זֹאת].

**20:6** And God said to him [וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו הָאֱלֹהִים] in the dream [בַּחֲלֹם]: I also [גַּם אָנֹכִי] — I know [יָדַעְתִּי] that in the integrity of your heart [כִּי בְתָם-לְבָבְךָ] you have done this [עָשִׂיתָ זֹאת] — and I held you back [וָאֶחְשֹׂךְ גַּם-אָנֹכִי אוֹתְךָ] also I — from sinning against me [מֵחֲטוֹ-לִי] — therefore [עַל-כֵּן] I did not let you touch her [לֹא-נְתַתִּיךָ לִנְגֹּעַ אֵלֶיהָ].

**20:7** And now [וְעַתָּה] return the wife of the man [הָשֵׁב אֵשֶׁת-הָאִישׁ] — for he is a prophet [כִּי-נָבִיא הוּא] — and he will pray for you [וְיִתְפַּלֵּל בַּעַדְךָ] and live [וֶחְיֵה] — but if you do not return [וְאִם-אֵינְךָ מֵשִׁיב] — know that you shall surely die [דַּע כִּי-מוֹת תָּמוּת] — you and all who are yours [אַתָּה וְכָל-אֲשֶׁר-לָךְ].

---

*Abimelech Confronts Abraham (vv. 8–10)*

**20:8** And Abimelech rose early in the morning [וַיַּשְׁכֵּם אֲבִימֶלֶךְ בַּבֹּקֶר] and he called [וַיִּקְרָא] to all his servants [לְכָל-עֲבָדָיו] and he spoke [וַיְדַבֵּר] all these words [אֵת כָּל-הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה] in their ears [בְּאָזְנֵיהֶם] and the men feared [וַיִּירְאוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים] greatly [מְאֹד].

**20:9** And Abimelech called [וַיִּקְרָא אֲבִימֶלֶךְ] to Abraham [לְאַבְרָהָם] and said to him [וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ]: What have you done to us [מֶה-עָשִׂיתָ לָּנוּ]? And in what have I sinned against you [וּמֶה-חָטָאתִי לָךְ] — that you have brought upon me [כִּי-הֵבֵאתָ עָלַי] and upon my kingdom [וְעַל-מַמְלַכְתִּי] a great sin [חֲטָאָה גְדֹלָה]? Deeds that are not done [מַעֲשִׂים אֲשֶׁר לֹא-יֵעָשׂוּ] — you have done to me [עָשִׂיתָ עִמָּדִי].

**20:10** And Abimelech said [וַיֹּאמֶר אֲבִימֶלֶךְ] to Abraham [אֶל-אַבְרָהָם]: What did you see [מָה רָאִיתָ] — that you did [כִּי עָשִׂיתָ] this thing [אֶת-הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה]?

---

*Abraham's Explanation — The Half-Sister Claim (vv. 11–13)*

**20:11** And Abraham said [וַיֹּאמֶר אַבְרָהָם]: Because I said [כִּי אָמַרְתִּי] — only [רַק] — there is no fear of God [אֵין-יִרְאַת אֱלֹהִים] in this place [בַּמָּקוֹם הַזֶּה] — and they will kill me [וַהֲרָגוּנִי] over the matter of [עַל-דְּבַר] my wife [אִשְׁתִּי].

**20:12** And also [וְגַם] — truly [אָמְנָה] she is my sister [אֲחֹתִי הִוא] — the daughter of my father [בַּת-אָבִי] but not the daughter of my mother [אַךְ לֹא בַת-אִמִּי] — and she became my wife [וַתְּהִי-לִי לְאִשָּׁה].

**20:13** And it came about [וַיְהִי] when God caused me to wander [כַּאֲשֶׁר הִתְעוּ אֹתִי אֱלֹהִים] from the house of my father [מִבֵּית אָבִי] — that I said to her [וָאֹמַר לָהּ]: This is your loyalty [זֶה חַסְדֵּךְ] that you shall do for me [אֲשֶׁר תַּעֲשִׂי עִמָּדִי] — to every place [אֶל כָּל-הַמָּקוֹם] where we go [אֲשֶׁר נָבוֹא שָׁמָּה] — say of me [אִמְרִי לִי]: He is my brother [אָחִי הוּא].

---

*Abimelech Restores Sarah — Gifts and Vindication (vv. 14–16)*

**20:14** And Abimelech took [וַיִּקַּח אֲבִימֶלֶךְ] sheep and cattle [צֹאן וּבָקָר] and male servants and female servants [וַעֲבָדִים וּשְׁפָחֹת] and gave [them] to Abraham [וַיִּתֵּן לְאַבְרָהָם] and returned to him [וַיָּשֶׁב לוֹ] Sarah his wife [אֵת שָׂרָה אִשְׁתּוֹ].

**20:15** And Abimelech said [וַיֹּאמֶר אֲבִימֶלֶךְ]: Look [הִנֵּה] — my land is before you [אַרְצִי לְפָנֶיךָ] — in what is good in your eyes [בַּטּוֹב בְּעֵינֶיךָ] dwell [שֵׁב].

**20:16** And to Sarah he said [וּלְשָׂרָה אָמַר]: Look [הִנֵּה] — I have given [נָתַתִּי] a thousand [pieces of] silver [אֶלֶף כֶּסֶף] to your brother [לְאָחִיךְ] — look [הִנֵּה] — it is for you a covering of the eyes [הוּא-לָךְ כְּסוּת עֵינַיִם] to all who are with you [לְכֹל אֲשֶׁר אִתָּךְ] and before all [וְאֵת כֹּל] — and she was reproved [וְנֹכָחַת].

---

*Abraham Prays — God Heals Abimelech's Household (vv. 17–18)*

**20:17** And Abraham prayed [וַיִּתְפַּלֵּל אַבְרָהָם] to God [אֶל-הָאֱלֹהִים] and God healed [וַיִּרְפָּא אֱלֹהִים] Abimelech [אֶת-אֲבִימֶלֶךְ] and his wife [וְאֶת-אִשְׁתּוֹ] and his female servants [וְאַמְהֹתָיו] and they gave birth [וַיֵּלֵדוּ].

**20:18** For the LORD [כִּי-עָצֹר עָצַר יְהוָה] had completely closed fast [עָצֹר עָצַר] every womb [בְּעַד כָּל-רֶחֶם] of the house of Abimelech [לְבֵית אֲבִימֶלֶךְ] on account of [עַל-דְּבַר] Sarah the wife of Abraham [שָׂרָה אֵשֶׁת אַבְרָהָם].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**On the Divine Name — Elohim vs. YHWH (vv. 1–18):**
This chapter employs *Elohim* (אֱלֹהִים) as the consistent name for God throughout vv. 3–17, making it the dominant divine title. The solitary exception is v. 18, where *YHWH* (יְהוָה) appears — rendered "the LORD" per project convention. The *Elohim*/*YHWH* distribution is a classic source-critical marker; this chapter is assigned by classical source criticism to the Elohist (E) strand. The Vaticanus LXX reads *ho theos* (ὁ θεός, "God") throughout, with *kyrios* (κύριος, "Lord") at v. 18, confirming the Hebrew pattern.

**20:1 — "he sojourned in Gerar" (*wayyāgār bigrār*):**
The verb *gur* (גּוּר) means to sojourn as a resident alien, not permanent settlement. It carries legal and social implications of foreignness. Gerar is in the northern Negev, associated in biblical tradition with Philistine territory (see Gen 26), though the anachronism of "Philistines" at this period is a longstanding scholarly question.

**20:2 — "said of Sarah his wife" (*wayyōmer ... 'el-śārāh 'ištô*):**
The preposition *'el* here is unusual; one expects *le* ("said to Sarah") or *'al* ("said concerning Sarah"). The text is slightly ambiguous: does Abraham say this *to* Sarah (instructing her) or does he say this *about* Sarah (to others)? The grammar favors "about" — he presented her to Abimelech as his sister. The LXX Vaticanus reads *peri Sarras* ("concerning Sarah"), supporting the sense "said about her."

**20:3 — "you are dead" (*hinnekha mēt*):**
The construction is a participle functioning as a predicate of imminent doom — literally "behold-you [are] dead [one]." The word *mēt* (מֵת) is the Qal active participle of *mût* (מוּת, "to die"). It conveys certainty of impending death, not a statement of current condition. Compare the same form in Gen 2:17 (*môt tāmût*, "dying you shall die").

**20:4 — "a righteous nation" (*gôy tsaddîq*):**
This phrase *gôy tsaddîq* (גּוֹי צַדִּיק) is arresting — Abimelech speaks as representative of his entire people, not merely himself. The echo of Abraham's intercession in Gen 18:23-25 (*tsaddîq... rāšā'*, "righteous... wicked") is unmistakable and likely intentional. The LXX reads *ethnos agnooûn* (ἔθνος ἀγνοοῦν, "an unknowing nation"), slightly softening the moral claim.

**20:5 — "integrity of my heart... innocence of my hands" (*bĕtom lĕbābî ûbĕniqyôn kappāy*):**
*Tom* (תֹּם) is moral wholeness or blamelessness. *Niqyôn* (נִקְיֹן) means cleanness, innocence — the only occurrence of this noun in the Pentateuch. Both hands and heart are invoked: the inward disposition and the outward action. A near-legal declaration of innocence.

**20:6 — "I held you back also I" (*wā'eḥśōk gam-'ānōkî 'ôtĕkā*):**
The emphatic double subject — *gam 'ānōkî* ("also I myself") — places stress on divine agency: it was God who restrained Abimelech, not Abimelech's own virtue. The verb *ḥāśak* (חָשַׂךְ) means to hold back, withhold, spare. The syntax is awkward in Hebrew, the emphatic pronoun being fronted mid-clause; I have preserved this feature.

**20:7 — "for he is a prophet" (*kî-nābî' hû'*):**
This is the first occurrence of *nābî'* (נָבִיא, "prophet") in the Hebrew Bible. Its use here is striking: Abraham is called a prophet not in connection with visions or oracles but specifically because he can intercede (*yitpallēl*, "he will pray") with God. The prophetic function here is intercessory, not predictive. The LXX has *prophētēs* (προφήτης), the standard Greek equivalent.

**20:9 — "deeds that are not done" (*ma'ăśîm 'ăšer lō'-yē'āśû*):**
The Niphal passive *yē'āśû* (יֵעָשׂוּ) functions as an ethical absolute: actions that are by definition not permissible. The indefinite plural parallels the same idiom in Gen 34:7 (the Dinah narrative), where it describes Shechem's violation. It is a formula of moral outrage.

**20:12 — the half-sister claim:**
Abraham claims Sarah is the "daughter of my father but not the daughter of my mother" (*bat-'ābî... lō' bat-'immî*). This raises the question of whether patriarchal marriage to a half-sibling was practiced; Lev 18:9 later prohibits it. The claim may be technically accurate (different mothers), or it may be a face-saving rationalization Abraham offers to Abimelech. The text does not editorially comment on its propriety.

**20:13 — "when God caused me to wander" (*ka'ăšer hit'û 'ōtî 'ĕlōhîm*):**
The verb *hit'û* (הִתְעוּ) is the Hiphil 3rd person *plural* of *tā'āh* (תָּעָה, "to wander, go astray"). With *Elohim* as subject, this creates a famous grammatical anomaly: *Elohim* takes a plural verb. Because *Elohim* is morphologically plural but grammatically singular when referring to the God of Israel, a plural verb is exceptional. Commentators differ: (a) archaic grammatical agreement with the plural form of the noun; (b) deliberate theological ambiguity; (c) evidence of a polytheistic source later monotheized. I have rendered it literally — "God caused me to wander" — without resolving the ambiguity. The LXX Vaticanus reads *exepesen me ho theos* (ἐξέπεσέν με ὁ θεός), with *ho theos* as a singular subject, normalizing the Greek.

**20:13 — "this is your loyalty" (*zeh ḥasdēk*):**
*Ḥesed* (חֶסֶד) is the rich covenant-loyalty word — often translated "steadfast love," "kindness," "faithfulness." Here Abraham deploys it in a personal request to Sarah: she is to demonstrate *ḥesed* (loyal commitment) by maintaining their cover story. The word oscillates between the divine sphere (God's covenant faithfulness) and the human (loyal conduct between people). I render it "loyalty" to preserve the relational weight without importing the theological register it carries in other contexts.

**20:16 — "covering of the eyes" (*kĕsût 'ênayim*):**
This phrase *kĕsût 'ênayim* (כְּסוּת עֵינַיִם) is among the most debated idioms in Genesis. Proposed readings include: (a) "a vindication before all" — the gift publicly clears Sarah's honor; (b) "a bribe to avert eyes" — money to ensure others look away and say nothing; (c) "a veil" in the sense of restoring her social standing as a covered/protected woman. The concluding *wĕnōkaḥat* (וְנֹכָחַת) — "and she was reproved" or "and she is [thus] vindicated/set right" — is itself ambiguous: the verb *yakah* (יָכַח, Niphal) can mean either "to be reproved/rebuked" or "to be vindicated/established as in the right." The LXX Vaticanus reads *kaì panta alētheusousa* ("and all things being set true/verified"), which leans toward vindication. I have translated the Hebrew literally and note that the ambiguity is unresolved.

**20:17–18 — "they gave birth" (*wayyēlēdû*) / "had completely closed fast" (*'ātsôr 'ātsar*):**
The verb *wayyēlēdû* (וַיֵּלֵדוּ) at v. 17 is 3rd plural — the female servants (and presumably Abimelech's wife) gave birth once healed. V. 18 uses the infinitive absolute construction *'ātsôr 'ātsar* (עָצֹר עָצַר) for intensive emphasis — "closing he had closed" — which I render "had completely closed fast." The pluperfect sense is required: the closing had occurred before the events of the chapter. Note that v. 18 uses *YHWH* rather than *Elohim*, the single use of the Tetragrammatic name in the chapter.

**LXX Vaticanus Observations (Pages 15–16):**
The Vaticanus text of Genesis 20 is well-preserved on these pages. Notable LXX variants: (a) v. 2, LXX adds *hoti autos mou adelphos estin* (explicit reciprocal "that he is my brother") — matching Hebrew. (b) v. 16, LXX considerably expands the speech to Abimelech with interpretive glosses. (c) The chapter division markers in the Vaticanus margin (*CAP. XXII* visible at page 15, *CAP. XXIII* at page 16) reflect the older Greek chapter enumeration, which differs from the modern Hebrew chapter numbering.

**DSS Note:**
Per the chapter manifest, no significant Dead Sea Scrolls fragments are extant for Genesis 20. The Leningrad Codex remains the sole primary Hebrew witness for this chapter.

— EMET, AI Translator | Literal Translation Division | Project AI Bible, 2025
